title: A (slightly deeper) look into the restricted 3-body problem
speaker: Agustin Moreno
abstract: In this talk, as a continuation of my talk in the Members’ Colloquium but with a specialized audience in mind, I will discuss in more detail some of the general geometric and dynamical structures underlying the theoretical aspects of the restricted 3-body problem, and outline new research directions.

proof_stages.label: Open-book, Lefschetz, and adapted contact input
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a five-dimensional contact manifold with an open book whose four-dimensional pages carry a Lefschetz fibration, together with contact forms on the manifold and binding adapted to these open-book structures.

proof_stages.label: Finite-energy foliation realization theorem
proof_stages.summary: The first main theorem asserts that the abstract open-book/Lefschetz data can be realized by a regular finite-energy holomorphic foliation whose projected moduli space is S^3 with an induced open book and whose restrictions to pages recover the given Lefschetz fibrations.

proof_stages.label: Holomorphic binding and page-compatible almost complex structure
proof_stages.summary: The proof begins by choosing an almost complex structure on the symplectization of the binding making its open book holomorphic, then extending it to the symplectization of the five-manifold so that the open-book pages become J-invariant hypersurfaces.

proof_stages.label: Intersection theory, Wendl filling theorem, and regularity
proof_stages.summary: Using Siefring-type intersection theory for holomorphic curves in hypersurfaces, Wendl’s theorem on planar fillings, and automatic transversality/block Fredholm arguments, the extended moduli space is shown to stay in the desired hypersurfaces, identify pagewise with the Lefschetz fibration, and be regular.

proof_stages.label: Obstruction to descending the original Reeb dynamics
proof_stages.summary: Although an adapted Reeb flow preserves the binding and is transverse to pages, its return map on a page need not preserve the Lefschetz foliation, so the original dynamics generally does not descend to a flow on the moduli space.

proof_stages.label: Shadowing cone on the moduli space
proof_stages.summary: Projecting the contact distribution along the holomorphic-foliation quotient gives a cone field on the S^3 moduli space, so original Reeb orbits project to cone orbits even though arbitrary cone orbits need not lift back upstairs.

proof_stages.label: Averaged contact form and projected Reeb field
proof_stages.summary: A one-form on the moduli space is defined by integrating the original contact form against the area form along each holomorphic curve, and an L^2 projection of the original Reeb field to the kernel of the linearized Cauchy–Riemann operator gives the Reeb vector field of this averaged contact form.

proof_stages.label: Cone representatives and dynamical comparison program
proof_stages.summary: The construction is packaged as a complete representative cone structure for the open book on S^3, with discussion of the one-way relation between upstairs and downstairs orbits, hoped-for entropy or measure-theoretic comparisons, and the interpretation of the cone as measuring failure of holomorphic/integrable preservation.

examples.note: The speaker points to the concrete page type occurring in the three-body problem, where the Lefschetz fiber is an annulus, to make the abstract open-book/Lefschetz input data visible.

examples.name: Round geodesic/Hopf-flow model on S^3
examples.note: For the round/Sasakian geodesic-flow model, the Reeb flow preserves the integrable structure and foliation, so the shadow cone collapses to a line and the shadow is just the Hopf flow.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker runs the shadow construction in the round/Sasakian S^3 model, identifying the return map as trivial, the cone as a line, and the resulting shadow as the Hopf flow

examples.name: Rotating Kepler problem as the mu=0 limit
examples.note: The integrable Kepler Hamiltonian in a rotating frame gives a mechanical model where the return map preserves annular fibers as classical twist maps and the shadow can be written explicitly, illustrating the intended use for perturbing toward the restricted three-body problem.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker writes the rotating Kepler Hamiltonian, decomposes it into Kepler energy and angular momentum terms, and analyzes the resulting return map, twist-map fibers, fixed polar collision orbits, and shadow dynamics

passes.reason: mu=0 is the boundary limit where the restricted three-body problem collapses to the integrable rotating Kepler two-body case
the rotating Kepler system is the integrable boundary case μ=0 where the restricted three-body problem loses the second primary

examples.name: Finite-periodic-orbit Reeb dynamics shadowing to an irrational ellipsoid
examples.note: In Q&A the speaker mentions a known contact-dynamical example on the same threefold with finitely many periodic orbits whose shadow is an irrational ellipsoid with two orbits, supporting the idea that the shadow records coarse Reeb dynamics.
